slug: 为什么旧游戏有一种新游戏给不了的感觉
title: 为什么旧游戏有一种新游戏给不了的感觉

outline.concept: Technical constraints created an imagination gap your brain had to fill. Low-res sprites and chiptune audio forced you to co-create the world — your brain treats self-generated details as personal memory, not received content. A 16×16 pixel face you interpreted as 'determined' sticks harder than a mocapped face showing determination, because you helped make it.
outline.query: 像素画面 脑补 想象力 sprite imagination 低分辨率

outline.concept: Pre-internet information scarcity made discoveries feel owned, not found. No wikis, no quest markers, no YouTube — beating a boss or finding a secret meant you held genuine rare knowledge. The endowment effect kicks in: you value what you struggled to earn, and that value tags the memory with weight that a looked-up solution never gets.
outline.query: 攻略 秘籍 隐藏 发现 没有网络 wiki cheat guide

outline.concept: Old games were short loops demanding repetition until fluent. Replaying the same stage 30 times builds procedural memory and genuine flow states — you don't just play, you become fluent. Modern games spread 60 hours of novel content; you're always a tourist, never a resident long enough to stop thinking and start flowing.
outline.query: 反复练习 通关 肌肉记忆 背板 mastery repetition flow

outline.concept: The feeling isn't stored in the game — it's stored in the context of who you were when you played it. Summer afternoons with zero responsibilities, a CRT's glow, the specific smell of your childhood room. The game is a retrieval cue for a whole self you've lost access to. Replaying the ROM doesn't work because you're querying the game, but the feeling was indexed under a person who no longer exists.
outline.query: 暑假 小时候 房间 怀念 childhood summer nostalgia CRT
